chunk_summary: This section discusses phenotype-based outpatient management for stunting, concurrent wasting, and stunting with overweight, emphasizing mortality risk prediction, tailored interventions, and determinant correction strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand phenotype-based classification for stunting and its role in predicting mortality risk.
Learn outpatient management protocols for stunting alone, concurrent wasting, and stunting with overweight.
Identify key determinants such as diet quality, anemia, and diarrhea prevention in stunting management.
Recognize the urgency of managing concurrent wasting and stunting (WaSt) using wasting protocols.
key_facts: Stunting is defined as HAZ < -2, while moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) is weight-for-height between -2 and -3 SD or MUAC 115–125 mm (6–59 months) without edema.
Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is weight-for-height < -3 SD and/or MUAC <115 mm and/or nutritional edema.
Concurrent wasting plus stunting (WaSt) is a high-mortality phenotype requiring wasting protocols for urgency and referral thresholds.
Stunting with overweight is classified as weight-for-height > +2 SD under 5 years or BMI-for-age in ages 5–19 years.
Outpatient care for stunting without wasting focuses on determinant correction, including diet quality, anemia treatment, and diarrhea prevention.
reference_quote: Managing 'undernutrition' as a single entity leads to missed risk. Use phenotype-based classification because it predicts mortality risk and required intensity.
document_key_topics: Phenotype-based classification for stunting and malnutrition
Outpatient management protocols for stunting and wasting
Double burden malnutrition: stunting with overweight
